description: Figure 4 plots strain hardening rate versus true stress for annealed 8Al steels (Fe-30.5Mn-8.0Al-1.2C, wt.%) tensile deformed at an initial strain rate of 5×10^-4 s^-1. The graph compares two heat-treatment conditions: the black curve represents the short-annealed sample (8Al-450°C/1h), while the red curve represents the long-annealed sample (8Al-600°C/24h). Both curves exhibit an initial sharp drop from approximately 5 GPa into a single hardening regime. The 450°C/1h condition stabilizes near 2 GPa before gradually declining beyond about 1200 MPa true stress. In contrast, the 600°C/24h condition maintains a comparable plateau near 2 GPa but persists to higher stresses, followed by a modest upturn and subsequent drop. This comparison demonstrates that prolonged annealing raises the flow stress range while sustaining moderate strain hardening capacity, supporting the conclusion that uniform intragranular cuboidal j-carbides formed during long-term annealing strengthen the matrix more effectively than the heterogeneous nanosized precipitates present in the short-annealed condition.
